data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-07T01:36:12.792Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating .NET Framework code that uses Thread.Abort to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort, Thread.Interrupt, or ThreadAbortException at all. It just uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work fine in modern .NET.

There's a `thread-abort-migration` skill available that specifically handles this. Let me check its description:

"DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


The skill explicitly warns against using it for this exact scenario. Since the code only relies on Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep—none of which need migration—I should clarify that no migration is actually required here. That said, I can still point the user toward modernization opportunities like switching to Task-based patterns and async-await instead of raw threading.
